title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the history and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors practiced kaitiakitanga through traditional resource management systems like rāhui and mataitai, and apply these principles to contemporary environmental challenges facing our local waterway.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices of environmental management used for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Analyze current environmental data to assess the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and identify one significant challenge
Collaboratively design a culturally-grounded action plan that applies mātauranga Māori principles to address an identified environmental issue
Present findings and proposals using appropriate te reo Māori terminology and cultural protocols

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa - Story of Our Stream
activities.description: Students begin with a guided visit to significant sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, listening to local kaumātua share pūrākau (traditional stories) about the stream's creation, its historical significance as a food source and transportation route, and its naming. Students create annotated maps identifying cultural and historical landmarks.

activities.title: Mātauranga Māori me te Kaitiakitanga - Traditional Knowledge and Guardianship
activities.description: Working with knowledge holders from local marae, students learn about traditional practices such as rāhui (temporary prohibition), monitoring moon cycles for fishing, and sustainable harvesting methods used by Ngāti Kahungunu. Students compare these practices with modern environmental management approaches.

activities.title: Tiaki Taiao - Environmental Investigation
activities.description: Students conduct water quality testing at three points along the stream, measuring pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and macroinvertebrate diversity. They document evidence of human impact and identify potential pollution sources, creating data visualizations that combine scientific measurements with cultural observations.

activities.title: Whakatau Take - Problem Solving Workshop
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze their collected data to identify the most pressing issue affecting the stream. They research how similar issues have been addressed using both mātauranga Māori and scientific approaches, then brainstorm potential solutions that honor cultural traditions while addressing modern challenges.

activities.title: Mahere Mahi - Action Plan Development
activities.description: Groups develop detailed action plans proposing specific, achievable interventions. Plans must include: cultural justification drawing on Ngāti Kahungunu traditions, practical implementation steps, required resources, community engagement strategies, and success measures. Students prepare to present plans to a panel including local kaumātua and council representatives.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu iwi environmental management plans and historical records
Local kaumātua and representatives from Tamatea Māori Wardens
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring tools and historical data
'Te Mana o te Wai' framework documents and implementation guidelines

tasks: Annotated cultural map of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with historical and cultural significance notes
Water quality data analysis report with identified environmental challenges
Group presentation of culturally-grounded action plan using appropriate te reo Māori terminology
Reflective journal documenting personal understanding of kaitiakitanga principles

nzcAlignment: Understand how people manage resources and environments (Level 5)
Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives (Level 5)
Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability (Level 5)
Understand how cultural practices vary but reflect similar purposes (Level 5)
